narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 99.2% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction—it's well below the average of around 6 for students who passed the bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, also lower than the typical 6 for successful bar takers, further reinforcing the model's concern about your early academic standing relative to peers. Your upper-middle family income played a smaller but still notable role in tipping the scales toward failure, as it didn't align as favorably with the profiles of most who passed compared to other brackets. While your LSAT score of 34 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 are below the averages of 37 and 3.24 for bar passers, and being full-time is common among them, these weren't the top drivers here. Overall, your profile compared to admitted students—who typically rank much higher in school deciles—signaled higher risk, even with your solid family background. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort; it's the model highlighting where your academic deciles deviated most from proven success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and focus on strategies to improve your class rankings in future coursework, perhaps through tutoring or study groups. Building a stronger undergraduate-like GPA in any additional classes could also help demonstrate growth. Reach out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback, and explore programs with bridge courses or bar prep support tailored for full-time students like you. With targeted improvements, you can reshape your profile for future applications and boost your real bar exam odds.
